explicit_text: I was told I was found abandoned just days after my birth in Seoul, South Korea. Later, I lived in the Ilsan orphanage in Seoul before being adopted. Although much of my early life there is unclear, Seoul is a significant place in my adoption story.
implicit_text: I was told I was found abandoned just days after my birth in the capital city of South Korea. Later, I lived in the Ilsan orphanage located in that same city before being adopted. Although much of my early life there is unclear, this metropolitan area is a significant place in my adoption story.

explicit_text: South Korea is my birth country, and learning about its history and culture has helped me replace the shame I was raised with by my adoptive family. I grew up hearing negative things about South Korea, but researching the Korean War and adoption history gave me a new perspective. I also discovered that South Korea was not part of the Hague Convention during my adoption.
implicit_text: The country where I was born is in East Asia, known for its rich history and culture, which helped me replace the shame I was raised with by my adoptive family. I grew up hearing negative things about this nation, but researching the mid-20th century conflict on the peninsula and adoption history gave me a new perspective. I also discovered that this country was not part of the Hague Convention during my adoption.

explicit_text: After being adopted by a military family, I was raised in the United States during the 1960s. My adoptive parents followed advice from the adoption agency to raise me as normally as possible without special attention. Growing up in the U.S., I struggled with shame about my Korean heritage until I began researching my adoption in 2008.
implicit_text: After being adopted by a military family, I was raised in North America during the 1960s. My adoptive parents followed advice from the adoption agency to raise me as normally as possible without special attention. Growing up in this country, I struggled with shame about my Korean heritage until I began researching my adoption in 2008.

explicit_text: Holt Children's Services was the adoption agency that brought me from South Korea to the United States. In 2008, I spent months researching my adoption through Holt, uncovering pieces of my past from the documents they provided. Although some information was incomplete or false, Holt was a key source in my journey to understand my origins.
implicit_text: The adoption agency that brought me from South Korea to the United States was a well-known organization specializing in international adoptions. In 2008, I spent months researching my adoption through them, uncovering pieces of my past from the documents they provided. Although some information was incomplete or false, this agency was a key source in my journey to understand my origins.

explicit_text: Understanding the Korean War was crucial to grasping the origins of international adoption from Korea, including my own story. The war's aftermath created many orphans and circumstances that led to adoptions abroad in the decades following. Learning about this history helped me make sense of why I was relinquished by my biological family.
implicit_text: Understanding the conflict on the Korean peninsula from 1950 to 1953 was crucial to grasping the origins of international adoption from that region, including my own story. The war's aftermath created many orphans and circumstances that led to adoptions abroad in the decades following. Learning about this history helped me make sense of why I was relinquished by my biological family.

explicit_text: I found out that South Korea was not part of the Hague Convention at the time of my adoption, which meant they were not required to authenticate adoption paperwork like birth records. This lack of regulation contributed to gaps and possible falsehoods in my adoption documents. Knowing this helped me understand why some information about my birth and early life was unreliable.
implicit_text: I found out that the East Asian country where I was born was not part of the international treaty governing adoption standards at the time of my adoption, which meant they were not required to authenticate adoption paperwork like birth records. This lack of regulation contributed to gaps and possible falsehoods in my adoption documents. Knowing this helped me understand why some information about my birth and early life was unreliable.

explicit_text: My adoption file mentioned that I possibly lived in Daegu until I was two years old before being moved to the Ilsan orphanage in Seoul. This city is part of my early childhood story, although details are scarce. It was from Daegu that I was transferred because I was deemed adoptable.
implicit_text: My adoption file mentioned that I possibly lived in a large southeastern city in South Korea until I was two years old before being moved to the Ilsan orphanage in the capital. This city is part of my early childhood story, although details are scarce. It was from this urban center that I was transferred because I was deemed adoptable.

explicit_text: My adoptive mother recalled that a friend I had from the Ilsan orphanage might have been adopted by another military family and moved to Minnesota. This lead gave me hope in 2009 when I tried to find her through Korean Bridge. Minnesota represents a possible connection to my past and someone who shared my early childhood.
implicit_text: My adoptive mother recalled that a friend I had from the Ilsan orphanage might have been adopted by another military family and moved to a Midwestern U.S. state known for its lakes. This lead gave me hope in 2009 when I tried to find her through a Korean adoptee support organization. That state represents a possible connection to my past and someone who shared my early childhood.

explicit_text: Korean Bridge is a non-profit organization that helps reunite orphans with their birth families. In 2009, I contacted John, a representative from Korean Bridge, who helped me appear on a Korean TV program to find my childhood friend and biological family. Although the show didn’t provide answers, Korean Bridge gave me support in my search.
implicit_text: A non-profit organization dedicated to reuniting orphans with their birth families helped me in 2009. I contacted a representative named John from this group, who helped me appear on a Korean TV program to find my childhood friend and biological family. Although the show didn’t provide answers, this organization gave me support in my search.
